videoTitle: Lesson 5.3- Funnel Mapping Basics (From Awareness to Conversion).mp4
transcript: Welcome to Lesson 5.3, Funnel Mapping Basics, From Awareness to Conversion. In Lesson 5.1, you learned the three funnel stages, TOFU, MOFU, and BOFU. In Lesson 5.2, you explored the five funnel types businesses use and when to recommend each one. Now it's time to learn how to take all of that strategy and turn it into an actual visual blueprint, a funnel map. By the end of this lesson, you will know what funnel mapping is, the five core elements every funnel map must include, how to connect those elements into a complete visual flow from awareness to conversion, and exactly how your map becomes the input for both your mirror diagrams in Lesson 5.4 and your go-high-level builds across modules 6 and 7. Let's get into it. So what exactly is funnel mapping? Funnel mapping is the process of visually documenting every step a prospect takes from first discovering a business all the way through to completing a conversion, including every touchpoint, every decision point, and every automation along the way. Think of it as the architectural blueprint for a building. Before a construction team pours a single foundation or installs a single window, an architect draws precise plans. Those plans tell every contractor what to build, in what order, and how each piece connects to everything else. Without them, the crew is guessing. That is exactly what happens when marketers skip funnel mapping and go straight to go high level. They build pages and automation in isolation, with no visual plan tying everything together. The result is a system with gaps. Leads that fall through because the follow-up sequence never triggers. Or pages that have no logical next step after the form is submitted. The principle is simple. Map first, build second. Every time a funnel map takes 30 to 60 minutes to create, It saves 5 to 10 hours of rebuilding and troubleshooting later inside Go High Level. That return on planning time is one of the most valuable habits you can develop as a marketing automation specialist. Every funnel map you create, regardless of the funnel type, must include five core elements. These are the building blocks of every visual funnel diagram you will draw in Miro in Lesson 5.4. Element number one is enter points. These are the channels that bring traffic into the top of your funnel, where prospects come from before they even reach your first page. Element number two is landing pages. These are the pages prospects land on at each conversion stage. The lead capture page, the thank you page, the sales or booking page. Element number three is touch points. These are the automated interaction that happen between pages. Welcome emails, nurture sequences, SMS follow-ups, and internal sales notifications. Element number four is decision points. These are the moments where a prospect's action determines which path they take next. Did they open the email, click the link, or book the call? Element number five is conversion goals. These are the specific actions that define success at each stage. The opt-in at Tofu, the engagement at Mofu, the booking or purchase at Bofu. Here's the critical rule. Missing even one of these five elements in your map creates a blind spot in your system. A map without decision points, for example, becomes a linear sequence that ignores what happens when someone does not take action. And that is where most leads are actually lost. Let me go deeper on element 1, enter points, because this is where many funnel maps are incomplete from the very beginning. An enter point is any channel that brings a cold or warm prospect into the top of your funnel. The most common enter points include paid ads from platforms like Facebook, Instagram, and Google, which you will learn in module 3 as outbound tactics. Organic Search Traffic from SEO Blog Content which is in the inbound approach for Module 3 Social media posts and content with links to your landing page Email links from an existing list clicking through to a new offer Referrals from word of mouth. Affiliate partners or joint ventures. And offline sources like business cards, print flyers, or events with a QR code. Here's why mapping entry points carefully matters. Different entry points often produce prospects at different temperatures. Someone who searched specifically for fitness coach for busy moms on Google is further along in their awareness than someone who saw a general interest ad while scrolling in Instagram. In some funnels, those two groups should land on different pages with messaging tailored to their awareness level. In Miro, your entry points will appear at the far left of your funnel diagram. They are the starting nodes that feed into the first landing page. When you have multiple entry points, draw each one separately and converge them at the landing page with connecting arrows. This visual clarity prevents the common mistake of designing one page that tries to speak to multiple audiences simultaneously. Now let us map elements 2 and 3. Landing pages and touch points. The two elements that carry the most volume of work in a typical funnel build. Every landing page in your funnel map should show three things. The offer on that page, the conversion action the visitor is asked to take, and where they go immediately after they take that action. In the lead generation funnel type from Lesson 5.2, you will typically map three landing pages. The lead capture page where the free offer is made and the form is submitted. This is the TOEFL conversion moment. The thank you page that immediately follows, confirms delivery, and gives the next step instructions. And the sales or booking page later in the funnel where the BOEFL conversion happens. Each of these walls built or referenced in Lesson 4.5 using the Go High-Level Funnel Builder. Your funnel map shows exactly which page appears in which sequence, ensuring the GHL build follows the same logical order. For touchpoints, you are mapping every automated interaction between those pages. The welcome email that fires at the moment a form is submitted is your first Tofu touchpoint. The nurture sequence of 3 to 7 emails over the following days is your move to touchpoints. You will build this in Module 6. If an SMS follow-up is part of the strategy, that is a touchpoint as well. Covered in Lesson 6.6 and 6.7. And in high-touch funnels, an internal sales team notification is a touchpoint that fires when a lead reaches a certain behavior threshold. You will build that in Module 7. In Miro, landing pages are typically represented as rectangles and touch points as oval or diamond shapes, all connected by directional arrows. Seeing them together in one diagram is what makes the system logic visible. Elements 4 and 5, decision points and conversion goals, are what transform a simple linear sequence into a true funnel system that responds intelligently to prospect behavior. A decision point is any moment where what the prospect does next determines which path they follow. The most powerful decision point in most MoFu sequences is a simple one. Did they click the booking link in the email or did they not? If yes, the system moves them to the booking page. If no, after a set number of days, a follow-up SMS or a different email goes out with a new angle or incentive. In Go High Level, this is built as an if-then conditional workflow using the triggers, actions, and conditions system you will learn in Lesson 7.2. In your funnel map, this is represented as a diamond shape with two arrows exiting it. One labeled Yes and one labeled No, each leading to a different next step. The rule that matters most here, always map both paths. The in-action path, the no-branch, is where the majority of your leads actually are at any given moment. A funnel map that only shows what happens when people take action is missing the most important question What happens when they do not For conversion goals remember that success is not just the final sale At Tofu the conversion goal is the opt A visitor becomes a lead At Mofu the conversion goal is meaningful engagement. A lead opens emails, click through content, and signals they are warming up. At Bofu, the conversion goal is the final action, a booking, a purchase, or an application. Map all three. Each stage conversion enables the one that follows. Let me show you what a complete funnel map looks like end-to-end using a practical example. Imagine you are building a lead generation funnel for a fitness coach who offers a free meal plan guide as her lead magnet. The map begins at the far left with empty points. There are two, a Facebook ad driving cold traffic and an Instagram bio link for organic social traffic. Both point with connecting arrows to the first landing page. The first landing page is the lead capture page for the free meal plan guide. This is a tofu landing page built in Lesson 4.5 using the Go High-Level Funnel Builder. The conversion goal at this stage is to form submission. the moment the visitor becomes a lead. That form submission is shown in the map as a small node labeled Form Submit, Conversion Goal 1. The form submission triggers two things simultaneously. The prospect is redirected to a thank you page that confirms the guide is on its way. And the first automated touchpoint fires, a welcome email that delivers the guide. This is a TOEFL automation build in GoHighLevel with the trigger to set form submitted. From the welcome email, the prospect enters a five-email nurture sequence. These are MOFU touchpoints built in Module 6. After the third email, a decision point appears. Did they click the discovery call booking link? If yes, they proceed to the booking page, which is a BOFU landing page. If no, a re-engagement SMS fires after three days with a new handle. I wanted to make sure you got the guide. Here is the link to book a call. This is the in-action path made visible in the map. Once they book, a booking confirmation and reminder workflow activates, which you will build in Lesson 7.5. That is the final conversion goal, a book discovery call. This entire journey is documented before a single page is built in Go High Level. That is the power of funnel mapping. In Lesson 5.4 and 5.5, you will draw this exact type of map inside Miro. Let me walk you through the five most common funnel mapping mistakes so you can avoid building a blueprint that creates problems during execution. Mistake number one, mapping only the happy path. Most beginners draw their funnel as a straight line from opt-in to conversion, showing only what happens when people take every action you want them to take. But most people do not. Always map both the yes and no branches at every decision point. The in-action path is not a failure. It is where your re-engagement strategy lives. Mistake number two, skipping the thank you page. Many beginners treat the thank you page as a formality and leave it out of the map entirely. But the thank you page is a strategic mofo touchpoint with a next step call to action. Map it explicitly. Build it intentionally in GoHighLevel's funnel builder as the second funnel step. And give it a specific goal. Book a call. Join a community. Watch a video. Mistake number three. 3. Mapping touchpoints without specifying the trigger. Writing send email 1 in your funnel map tells you nothing about when or why the email fires. Every touchpoint in your map must show what triggers it. A form submission, a link click, a time delay of 2 days, or a specific tag being applied in Go High 11. Mistake number 4. Creating one funnel for all traffic sources without reviewing whether that is appropriate. If your Facebook ad drives cold traffic and your email list drives warmer engagement traffic, those two audiences may need different first page experiences. Your map reveals whether one page serves both or whether you need separate pages Mistake number five Building and go high level before the funnel map is complete It tempting to start clicking inside the platform because it feels like progress It's not. A partial map produces a partial automation, and the hours spent rebuilding and reconnecting broken sequences in GHL far exceed the 30 to 60 minutes required to finish the map first. Your funnel map is not just a planning document. It's a direct instruction set for what to build in Go High Level and in what order. Let me show you how each map element translates to specific GHL build action. Step number one maps directly to configuring your traffic sources, your entry points, the Facebook ad, the Instagram bio link, the SEO content. Each need a URL pointing to your landing page. This is the final connection from Module 3 campaign work to your funnel. Step number 2 corresponds to building your lead capture landing page using Go High-Level Funnel Builder from Lesson 4.5. Your map shows you exactly what offer appears on the page, what form to include, and where the visitor goes after submitting. Step number three is building the thank you page as the second funnel step inside GoHighLevel. Also covered in lesson 4.5. Your map tells you what confirmation message to show and what the next step action is. Steps number four covers Tofu and Mofu touchpoints. Specifically, the welcome email and nurture sequence you will build in module 6 using GoHighLevel's email campaign builder. Your map specifies the trigger for each email and the number of messages in the sequence. Step number five, cover building the decision point logic in GHL. This becomes a conditional workflow using the if-then automation builder you will learn in lesson 7.2. Your map shows the condition. Did they click the link? And the two branches. What happens if yes and what happens if no? Step number six is building the BOFU, conversion page, the booking or sales page, again using the GHL funnel builder. And the booking confirmation and reminder workflow reference in lesson 7.5 completes the system. Every step in Go High Level traces back directly to a node on your funnel map. That is not coincidence, it's by design. The map is the plan. Go High Level is the execution. Let's lock in what you learned in this lesson. Funnel mapping is the process of visually documenting every step from discovery to conversion before opening Go high level. It's the blueprint that prevents rebuilds, gaps, and misaligned automations. Every funnel map must include all five core elements. Enter points, landing pages, touch points, decision points, and conversion goals. Missing even one creates a blind spot that loses leads. Entry points are the channels bringing traffic in. Map all of them. Because different traffic sources may require different first experiences. Decision points create yes and no branches in your map. Always map both paths. The in-action path is where most leads are and where your re-engagement strategy must live. Conversion goals exist at every funnel stage. TOEFL opt-in, MOFU engagement, and BOFU booking or purchase. Each stage conversion enables the next. And your completed funnel map becomes the direct input for Miro in Lesson 5.4 and 5.5. And the build sequence for every Go High Level workflow you create across Module 6 and 7. Coming up next is Lesson 5.4. Introduction to Miro for Funnel Visualization You now know the five elements of a funnel map and how to connect them into a complete strategic blueprint. The next lesson introduces the tool you will use to draw the blueprint, Miro. You will learn how to navigate a Miro board, use the shapes and connectors that brings your funnel to life, and apply the visual language that makes your diagram immediately understandable to clients and colleagues. Miro is where your funnel strategy becomes visible. And Lesson 5.5 is where you build the complete funnel from scratch inside it using everything from this lesson as your foundation. Let's continue to Lesson 5.4.

visualDescriptions.description:
The image presents a slide from an online course or presentation focused on funnel mapping, specifically titled "Funnel Mapping Basics: From Awareness to Conversion." The slide is structured to guide viewers through the process of creating a visual blueprint for their funnel strategy before implementing it in GoHighLevel.

**Title and Subtitle:**
- **Title:** Funnel Mapping Basics: From Awareness to Conversion
- **Subtitle:** Turn your funnel strategy into a visual blueprint before you build anything in GoHighLevel

**Key Learning Objectives:**
The slide outlines what viewers will learn, categorized under "WHAT YOU'LL LEARN," which includes:
- What funnel mapping is and why it prevents costly build mistakes
- The 5 core elements every funnel map must include
- How to map TOFU, MOFU, and BOFU stages into a connected flow
- Identifying entry points, touchpoints, and conversion moments
- How your map becomes the blueprint for Miro and GoHighLevel

**Visual Representation:**
A flowchart on the right side of the slide illustrates the basic stages of a funnel:
1. **Entry Point**
2. **Landing Page**
3. **Nurture**
4. **Convert**

Each stage is represented by a box with an icon and text, connected by lines to show the progression from one stage to the next. The "Convert" stage is highlighted in green, indicating completion or success.

**Additional Details:**
- **Lesson Number:** Lesson 5.3
- **Estimated Time:** 14 minutes
- **Website:** www.genspark.ai (with a note on how to exit full-screen mode)
- **Logo:** LIBRE, with a green leaf symbol above the "I" in "LIBRE"

This structured approach helps viewers understand the fundamental steps involved in creating an effective funnel strategy, emphasizing the importance of planning and visualization before execution.

The image shows a man speaking in front of the camera, with his hands raised as if he is explaining something. 

* The man is wearing a tan-colored shirt and has short black hair.
	+ He appears to be sitting in a room with a shelf behind him.
* There are various items on the shelf and walls around him, including:
	+ A guitar hanging on the wall to the left of the man.
	+ A bookshelf with books and other items on it to the left of the man.
	+ A light fixture with a yellow light bulb to the left of the man.
	+ A poster or picture on the wall behind the man.
	+ A shelf with a small object on it to the right of the man.
* The background of the image is a white wall with a greenish tint.

The overall atmosphere of the image suggests that the man is recording a video or giving a presentation, possibly in a home office or study setting.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to understanding the five core elements of every funnel map, which are essential for creating effective marketing strategies. The title, "The 5 Core Elements of Every Funnel Map," is prominently displayed at the top, followed by a brief description that emphasizes the importance of including all five elements to avoid blind spots.

**The Five Core Elements:**

*   **Entry Points**
    *   Where does traffic come from? (Ads, SEO, social, referrals?)
*   **Landing Pages**
    *   What page do they land on? What is the offer being made?
*   **Touchpoints**
    *   What emails, SMS, or follow-ups happen after they opt in?
*   **Decision Points**
    *   Where do they choose to continue or exit? What triggers next step?
*   **Conversion Goals**
    *   What is the final action — booking, purchase, registration?

**Additional Information:**

*   A note at the bottom of the image provides additional context, stating that in Miro (Lesson 5.4), each of these 5 elements becomes a specific shape or sticky note in your visual map.

In summary, the image provides a clear and concise overview of the five core elements of every funnel map, highlighting their importance in creating effective marketing strategies. By understanding and incorporating these elements, businesses can better navigate their customer journey and achieve their conversion goals.

The image presents a comprehensive overview of entry points, which are channels or sources that bring potential customers into the top of a sales funnel. The title "Element 1: Entry Points" is prominently displayed at the top, followed by a definition and six distinct categories of entry points.

*   **Definition**
    *   An entry point is any channel or source that brings a cold or warm prospect into the top of your funnel. It's the "start" button for your customer journey.
*   **Paid Ads**
    *   Facebook
    *   Instagram
    *   Google
    *   TikTok (Module 3 outbound traffic)
*   **Email Links**
    *   Existing list clicks through to a new landing page offer
*   **Organic Search**
    *   SEO blog content
    *   YouTube videos (Module 3 inbound traffic)
*   **Referrals**
    *   Word of mouth
    *   Affiliate links
    *   Partner traffic
*   **Social Media**
    *   Posts
    *   Reels
    *   Stories with "Link in Bio" or direct CTAs
*   **Offline Sources**
    *   Business cards
    *   Flyers
    *   Event QR codes scanning to URLs

The image provides a clear and concise visual representation of the various entry points that can lead potential customers into a sales funnel. By understanding these different channels and sources, businesses can better optimize their marketing strategies to effectively reach and engage with their target audience. Overall, the image serves as a valuable resource for marketers and business owners looking to improve their customer acquisition efforts. 

**MIRO MAPPING TIP:** Entry points appear at the far left of your diagram - they are the starting nodes of every funnel branch. Map ALL of them so no traffic source is unaccounted for.

The image presents a comprehensive overview of the key elements involved in creating effective landing pages and touchpoints, which are crucial components of a marketing strategy. The title, "Elements 2 and 3: Landing Pages and Touchpoints," sets the stage for the detailed information that follows.

**Landing Pages**

*   **Mapping Rule:** Each landing page must show:
    *   The Offer
    *   The Conversion Action
    *   The Next Step
*   **Types of Landing Pages:**
    *   **Lead Capture Page:** Collects name and email (built in Lesson 4.5)
    *   **Thank You Page:** Confirms delivery and gives next step instruction
    *   **Sales/Booking Page:** Drives the bottom-of-funnel (BOFU) conversion
*   **Miro Representation:** Rectangles in your diagram with the page name and conversion goal labeled inside.

**Touchpoints**

*   **Definition:** The automated interactions between pages
*   **Types of Touchpoints:**
    *   **Welcome Email:** Triggered immediately when form is submitted
    *   **Nurture Sequence:** 3 to 7 emails delivered over days or weeks (Module 6)
    *   **SMS Follow-up:** Time-sensitive message for quick engagement (Mod 6.6)
    *   **Internal Notification:** Alerts sales team of hot lead (Module 7)
*   **Miro Representation:** Diamond or oval shapes connected by arrows in between the landing pages.

In summary, the image provides a clear and structured guide to understanding the essential elements of landing pages and touchpoints. By following these guidelines, marketers can create effective strategies to engage with their audience, drive conversions, and ultimately achieve their marketing goals.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to understanding decision points and conversion goals in the context of sales funnels. The title, "Elements 4 and 5: Decision Points and Conversion Goals," is prominently displayed at the top.

**Decision Points**

* Definition: A decision point is any moment where the prospect's action determines which path they take next in their journey.
* Examples:
	+ Did they open the email? (Yes/No)
	+ Did they click the booking link? (Yes/No)
	+ Did they purchase? (Yes/No)
* Rule: Every decision point creates two branches - an action path and an inaction path.

**Conversion Goals**

* Definition: What success looks like at each stage.
* Types:
	+ **TOFU (Top of Funnel)**: Opt-in Conversion - Visitor submits form on lead capture page. Success = New Contact created.
	+ **MOFU (Middle of Funnel)**: Engagement Conversion - Lead opens emails and clicks through to content. Success = Engaged Lead.
	+ **BOFU (Bottom of Funnel)**: Booking or Purchase - Lead completes the final action. Success = Customer or Opportunity.
* Rule: Your funnel map must show the conversion goal at EACH stage - not just the final sale at the end.

**Additional Notes**

* Miro Note: Decision points are often represented as diamond shapes with two arrows exiting - one for "Yes" and one for "No".
* Miro Note: Mark conversion goals clearly (e.g., green star or flag icon) to show exactly where value is captured.

In summary, the image provides a clear and concise explanation of decision points and conversion goals, highlighting their importance in understanding customer behavior and optimizing sales funnels. By identifying decision points and setting conversion goals, businesses can create a more effective and efficient sales process.

The image presents a comprehensive flowchart illustrating the entire customer journey, from initial awareness to conversion, for a fitness coach offering a free meal plan guide. The flowchart is titled "A Complete Funnel Map: Annotated Example" and features a scenario specific to a fitness coach.

**Flowchart Components:**

*   **Facebook Ad (Entry Point)**
    *   The journey begins with a Facebook ad serving as the entry point.
    *   This ad directs users to a lead capture page.
*   **Lead Capture Page (TOFU Landing Page)**
    *   The lead capture page is designed to collect user information.
    *   Users are prompted to submit a form to access the free meal plan guide.
*   **Thank You Page**
    *   After submitting their information, users are redirected to a thank-you page.
    *   This page likely provides additional information or resources related to the free meal plan guide.
*   **Welcome Email (Auto-Triggered)**
    *   An auto-triggered welcome email is sent to users after they opt-in.
    *   This email likely includes a link to download the free meal plan guide and may contain additional information or promotions.
*   **5-Email Nurture**
    *   A series of five emails are sent to users to nurture them and build trust.
    *   These emails may provide valuable content, testimonials, or promotions to encourage users to take the next step.
*   **Booking Page**
    *   Users who click on a specific link in one of the nurture emails are directed to a booking page.
    *   This page allows users to schedule a consultation or appointment with the fitness coach.
*   **Re-engage SMS (Inaction Path)**
    *   Users who do not click on the link or take action are sent a re-engagement SMS.
    *   This message aims to re-engage users and encourage them to take the next step.

**Key Takeaways:**

*   The flowchart illustrates a well-structured customer journey that guides users from awareness to conversion.
*   Each step in the journey is designed to build trust, provide value, and encourage users to take the next step.
*   The use of multiple touchpoints, including emails and SMS, helps to nurture users and increase the likelihood of conversion.

**Conclusion:**

The flowchart provides a clear and concise visual representation of the customer journey for a fitness coach offering a free meal plan guide. By understanding each step in the journey, businesses can create effective marketing strategies that drive conversions and grow their customer base.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to avoiding common mistakes in funnel mapping, specifically tailored for users of GoHighLevel. The title, "5 Funnel Mapping Mistakes to Avoid," sets the tone for the informative content that follows.

Here are the main points:

• **Mapping only the happy path - ignoring inaction**
	+ Description: Focusing solely on the successful journey of users through a funnel, neglecting those who may not complete the desired actions.
	+ Solution: Always map Yes and No branches at every decision point
	+ Statistics: No statistics provided

• **Skipping the Thank You page in the map**
	+ Description: Omitting the Thank You page from the funnel map, which can lead to missed opportunities for further engagement.
	+ Solution: Map it explicitly as a MOFU touchpoint with next-step CTA
	+ Statistics: No statistics provided

• **Mapping touchpoints without specifying triggers**
	+ Description: Failing to define specific triggers for touchpoints, leading to unclear or ineffective funnel mappings.
	+ Solution: Define specific triggers: form submit, link click, delay, or tag
	+ Statistics: No statistics provided

• **Creating one single funnel for all traffic sources**
	+ Description: Using a single funnel for all traffic sources, which can result in a lack of personalization and reduced effectiveness.
	+ Solution: Map entry points separately and converge later if appropriate
	+ Statistics: No statistics provided

• **Building in GoHighLevel before map is complete**
	+ Description: Constructing a funnel in GoHighLevel before finalizing the map, which can lead to unnecessary rework and inefficiencies.
	+ Solution: Complete the full map in Miro before opening GHL
	+ Statistics: A funnel map takes 30 to 60 minutes to create. It saves 5 to 10 hours of rebuilding inside GoHighLevel.

In summary, the image provides valuable insights into common pitfalls to avoid when creating funnel maps in GoHighLevel. By being aware of these mistakes and following the recommended solutions, users can create more effective and efficient funnels that drive better results.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to building a funnel map in GoHighLevel, titled "From Funnel Map to GoHighLevel: The Build Sequence." The title is prominently displayed at the top, followed by a subtitle that reads, "Your map tells you exactly what to build - and in what order."

The main content of the image is organized into six numbered sections, each representing a step in the build sequence. These steps are:

*   **Entry Points**
    *   GHL ACTION: Configure traffic sources & set up ad links
    *   Module 3
*   **Lead Capture Page**
    *   GHL ACTION: Build landing page in Funnel Builder
    *   Lesson 4.5
*   **Thank You Page**
    *   GHL ACTION: Build confirmation page as second funnel step
    *   Lesson 4.5
*   **Touchpoints**
    *   GHL ACTION: Build email sequences in Campaigns
    *   Module 6
*   **Decision Points**
    *   GHL ACTION: Build conditional workflows in Automation
    *   Module 7.2
*   **BOFU Conversion**
    *   GHL ACTION: Build booking/sales page & workflows
    *   Lesson 7.5

At the bottom of the image, a black button with white text reads, "This sequence is the foundation of the Capstone Project in Module 9." The background of the image features a white grid pattern, providing a clean and organized visual representation of the build sequence.

Overall, the image provides a clear and concise roadmap for building a funnel map in GoHighLevel, making it an effective tool for users looking to create a successful marketing funnel.
